primary_diagnosis.name: Acromegaly due to a growth-hormone-secreting pituitary macroadenoma, now with mass effect (optic chiasm compression causing bitemporal hemianopia) and pituitary apoplexy risk

comorbidities.name: Bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic autonomous growth-hormone (GH) hypersecretion, almost always from a somatotroph pituitary adenoma, drives hepatic IGF-1 production. Sustained GH/IGF-1 excess causes the insidious acral and soft-tissue overgrowth (enlarging hands/feet, coarse facies, prognathism, macroglossia, widened tooth spacing), acromegalic arthropathy, hyperhidrosis, skin oiliness and vocal-cord thickening (deep voice) seen over the preceding two years. As the adenoma enlarges into a macroadenoma it extends supra-sellarly and compresses the optic chiasm, producing the classic bitemporal (peripheral) visual field loss, and can outgrow its blood supply or bleed — pituitary apoplexy — presenting with acute severe headache, rapidly progressive visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, and potentially acute hypopituitarism and haemodynamic collapse. The acute crescendo headache with new fixed bitemporal field loss in this patient is the time-critical mass-effect emergency; permanent visual loss can result from delayed decompression. Definitive confirmation requires serum IGF-1, an oral glucose tolerance test with GH suppression, an anterior pituitary hormone profile and MRI of the pituitary — none obtainable by telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Pituitary mass (acromegaly / macroadenoma) with optic chiasm compression
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic acral/facial overgrowth plus new bitemporal visual field loss and worsening headache points to a supra-sellar mass compressing the chiasm.
Headache with visual symptoms — but a FIXED bitemporal field defect and prominent somatic change argue against migraine.
Acute severe headache plus rapidly progressive visual loss on a background macroadenoma is the apoplexy pattern — a neurosurgical emergency to actively exclude.

differential.diagnosis: Acromegaly / GH-secreting macroadenoma with optic chiasm compression
differential.evidence_basis: Two-year acral/facial change + arthropathy + new bitemporal hemianopia is a coherent mass-effect picture requiring urgent imaging and hormone workup.
No thunderclap onset, vomiting or altered consciousness yet — apoplexy not established but cannot be excluded remotely; must be safety-netted aggressively.
Fixed peripheral field loss, absence of scintillating scotoma, and the somatic changes make primary migraine untenable as the explanation.
No symmetrical small-joint polyarthritis or prolonged early-morning stiffness; the acral/facial morphological change points elsewhere.
Macroglossia can coexist, but OSA does not explain the acute headache and visual field defect driving tonight's presentation.

differential.diagnosis: Acromegaly (GH-secreting pituitary macroadenoma) with optic chiasm compression, apoplexy to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Coherent chronic-plus-acute mass-effect picture; diagnosis is presumptive clinically and cannot be confirmed by telehealth — needs urgent MRI, IGF-1/GH and pituitary hormone profile plus formal visual fields.

red_flags.description: New bitemporal (peripheral) visual field loss — optic chiasm compression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chiasmal compression threatens permanent visual loss; requires urgent imaging and same-day neuro-ophthalmology / neurosurgical assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible blindness if decompression is delayed.

red_flags.description: Acute crescendo severe headache superimposed on a chronic pituitary mass
red_flags.clinical_significance: A change in headache pattern in a suspected macroadenoma raises expanding mass / early apoplexy and warrants urgent evaluation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed impending apoplexy with acute visual loss, hypopituitarism or collapse.

red_flags.description: Acute inability to mobilise safely — high fall risk with combined visual and joint impairment
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks the severity of the acute decline and an immediate safety hazard needing support and supervision tonight.

red_flags.description: Apoplexy features screened — thunderclap onset, vomiting, drowsiness / altered consciousness currently absent
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their appearance would convert this to an immediate 000 emergency; their current absence does not lower the need for urgent same-day assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the headache and visual symptoms to migraine with aura and reassuring — missing the fixed bitemporal field loss that signals chiasmal compression.
Being drawn to the slow two-year acral changes and treating the encounter as a routine endocrine referral, under-weighting the acute vision-threatening mass effect.
Focusing on the joint pain as the presenting problem and organising rheumatology/physio while overlooking the neuro-ophthalmic emergency.

Attempting to 'confirm acromegaly' or exclude apoplexy remotely — neither is possible without IGF-1/GH, pituitary hormones, formal perimetry and MRI; the correct move is urgent in-person assessment and imaging.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal visual field / confrontation testing and fundoscopy
Exact blood pressure and other measured vitals
Serum IGF-1, GH (with OGTT suppression) and anterior pituitary hormone profile
MRI of the pituitary and the degree of optic chiasm compression or any haemorrhage
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a probable pituitary mass with new optic chiasm compression; arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department given the new visual field loss) with urgent MRI pituitary and urgent endocrinology + neurosurgery referral; safety-net explicitly for pituitary apoplexy (000 if thunderclap headache, rapid/complete vision loss, intractable vomiting or altered consciousness).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely; the real risk is UNDER-action — anchoring on migraine or arthritis and under-triaging, or failing to escalate the new visual field loss.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Acromegaly (2014)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Confirm GH excess biochemically (IGF-1, GH nadir on OGTT) and image the pituitary with MRI; assess visual fields when a macroadenoma abuts the optic chiasm.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Pituitary Society consensus on pituitary apoplexy
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute headache with visual deterioration on a background pituitary adenoma requires emergency imaging, corticosteroid cover and urgent neurosurgical/endocrine involvement.
